rupture.definition: A break or tear, especially in a relationship caused by hurt or misunderstanding.
rupture.example: Every relationship will have some rupture at times.

repair.definition: To fix something that was broken or damaged.
repair.example: It is important to repair your relationship after a fight.

ideal.example: In an ideal world, no one would get hurt.

acted.definition: Did something based on a feeling or thought.
acted.example: She acted on her anger without thinking.

inappropriately.definition: In a way that is not suitable or correct for the situation.
inappropriately.example: He responded inappropriately to the child's request.

threaten.definition: To say you will do something bad to someone if they don't do what you want.
threaten.example: Parents should not threaten their children.

unrealistic.definition: Not possible or practical in real life.
unrealistic.example: It is unrealistic to never make mistakes.

experienced.definition: Having a lot of skill or knowledge from doing something for a long time.
experienced.example: Even an experienced parent makes mistakes.

fury.example: She acted on her fury without thinking.

mend.definition: To fix or heal something that is broken.
mend.example: We need to mend the hurt between us.

inevitable.definition: Certain to happen; cannot be avoided.

intimate.definition: Very close and personal, with deep feelings of connection.
intimate.example: Intimate relationships require trust and honesty.

familial.definition: Related to or typical of a family.
familial.example: Familial bonds are some of the strongest.

recognize.definition: To identify or notice something you have seen or experienced before.
recognize.example: Learn to recognize your emotional triggers.

triggers.definition: Things that cause a strong emotional reaction, often from past experiences.
triggers.example: Understanding your triggers helps you react better.

apologize.definition: To say you are sorry for something you did wrong.
apologize.example: It is important to apologize when you make a mistake.

moons.definition: Here means months; 'many moons' means a long time ago.
moons.example: Even many moons after it happened, you can still apologize.

tremendous.definition: Very great in amount, size, or importance.
tremendous.example: An apology can mean a tremendous amount to a child.

belief.definition: Something you accept as true or real.
belief.example: She carried the belief that her mother didn't care.

assumed.definition: Believed something to be true without proof.
assumed.example: The child assumed her mother did not love her.

relief.definition: A feeling of happiness when something bad stops or does not happen.
relief.example: It was a relief to learn the truth.

merely.example: She had merely been confused, not uncaring.

muddle.definition: A state of confusion or disorder.
muddle.example: Her mother had been in a muddle about her feelings.

dangerous.definition: Able to cause harm or hurt.
dangerous.example: Is it dangerous to apologize to children?

secure.example: Children need to feel secure with their parents.

authentic.definition: Real and genuine; true to who you really are.
authentic.example: Children need authentic, not perfect, parents.

instincts.definition: Natural feelings that guide behavior without conscious thought.

tune.definition: Here means in harmony or agreement with someone's feelings.
tune.example: Are you in tune with your child?

pretend.definition: To act as if something is true when it is not.
pretend.example: Don't pretend that everything is fine when it's not.

dull.definition: To make something less sharp or sensitive.
dull.example: Pretending will dull their natural instincts.

overadapts.definition: Changes too much to please others, losing their own sense of self.
overadapts.example: A child who overadapts may lose their own identity.

vulnerable.definition: Easily hurt or harmed, physically or emotionally.
vulnerable.example: Children are vulnerable and need protection.

interests.definition: Things that are good for someone; their benefit or well-being.
interests.example: Act in your child's best interests.

instinct.definition: A natural feeling that helps you know what to do without thinking.
instinct.example: Trust your instinct when something feels wrong.

component.definition: An important part of something larger.
component.example: Instinct is a major component of confidence.

competence.definition: The ability to do something well.

intelligence.definition: The ability to learn, understand, and think clearly.
intelligence.example: Emotional intelligence is as important as academic intelligence.

workshop.definition: A meeting where people learn about a topic through discussion and activities.

attend.definition: To go to an event or meeting.
attend.example: His wife suggested he attend the workshop.

agreed.definition: Had the same opinion; made a decision together.
agreed.example: They agreed not to have children at first.

IVF.definition: A medical process to help people have a baby when they cannot naturally (In Vitro Fertilization).
IVF.example: After a year of IVF, she got pregnant.

pregnant.definition: Having a baby growing inside the body.
pregnant.example: She got pregnant after IVF treatment.

miraculously.definition: In a way that seems like a miracle; amazingly.
miraculously.example: On TV, the baby is miraculously always asleep.

cot.definition: A small bed with high sides for a baby (British English for crib).
cot.example: The baby slept peacefully in the cot.

spontaneity.definition: The quality of doing things suddenly without planning.
spontaneity.example: Having a baby means losing some spontaneity.

flexibility.definition: The ability to change easily and adapt to different situations.
flexibility.example: Parenting requires giving up some flexibility.

tedium.definition: The state of being very boring and lasting a long time.
tedium.example: The tedium of daily baby care can be overwhelming.

duty.definition: Something you are required to do as part of your job or role.
duty.example: One parent is always on baby duty.

swing.definition: To move back and forth between two different feelings or states.
swing.example: He would swing between feeling resentful and depressed.

resentful.definition: Feeling bitter and angry about something you think is unfair.
resentful.example: He felt resentful about losing his freedom.

depressed.definition: Feeling very sad, hopeless, and without energy for a long time.
depressed.example: After the baby was born, he felt depressed.

reverts.definition: Goes back to an earlier or original state.
reverts.example: The conversation always reverts to the baby.

selfish.definition: Caring only about yourself and not thinking about others.
selfish.example: He felt selfish for wanting time alone.

fuse.definition: Here in 'short fuse' means getting angry very quickly.
fuse.example: He was on a short fuse with everyone.

exploring.definition: Looking at something carefully to learn about it.
exploring.example: He wasn't interested in exploring his childhood.

clue.definition: A piece of information that helps you understand something.
clue.example: His resistance was a clue to his pain.

distance.definition: To separate yourself from something emotionally.
distance.example: He wanted to distance himself from his past.

suspected.definition: Believed something was true based on evidence but without being certain.
suspected.example: I suspected he was hiding painful feelings.

frequent.example: His father's visits became less frequent.

disappearance.definition: The act of going away and not being seen anymore.
disappearance.example: The disappearance of his father affected him deeply.

desertion.definition: The act of leaving someone who depends on you, permanently.
desertion.example: His father's desertion left deep emotional scars.

painful.example: It was too painful to remember his father leaving.

unlock.definition: To open something that was locked; here means to allow hidden feelings to come out.
unlock.example: He needed to unlock his box of difficult emotions.

sensitive.definition: Able to understand and respond to other people's feelings.
sensitive.example: A parent needs to be sensitive to their child's needs.

dismissing.definition: Treating something as not important; ignoring it.
dismissing.example: Rather than dismissing his sadness, he sought help.

therapy.definition: Treatment of emotional or mental problems by talking with a trained professional.
therapy.example: Therapy helped him understand his anger.

surprise.example: To his surprise, he started crying in therapy.

crying.definition: Producing tears because of strong emotions like sadness or pain.
crying.example: He found himself crying about his father.

shouting.definition: Speaking very loudly, often because of anger.
shouting.example: He was shouting about his father leaving.

discontent.definition: A feeling of not being satisfied; unhappiness.
discontent.example: His discontent turned to hope after therapy.

gratitude.definition: A feeling of being thankful and appreciative.
gratitude.example: He felt gratitude for his family.

foundations.definition: The basic parts on which something is built or depends.
foundations.example: They were laying the foundations of a great relationship.

valuable.definition: Very useful, important, or worth a lot.

freed.definition: Made someone able to do something by removing a restriction.
freed.example: His change freed his wife to think about other things.

attitude.definition: The way you think and feel about something.
attitude.example: He changed his attitude towards being a parent.

grief.definition: Very deep sadness, especially after losing someone important.
grief.example: He could not unlock his love until he had unlocked his grief.

desire.definition: A strong wish or want to do something.
desire.example: His desire to leave was rooted in his past.

desert.definition: To leave someone who depends on you; to abandon them.
desert.example: He did not want to desert his son.

hazy.definition: Not clear; vague and unclear in your mind.
hazy.example: His idea of parenthood was hazy before the baby came.

parenthood.definition: The state of being a parent.
parenthood.example: Parenthood is harder than most people expect.

misunderstand.definition: To not understand correctly; to get the wrong meaning.
misunderstand.example: Ruptures happen when we misunderstand each other.

moods.definition: The way you feel at a particular time (happy, sad, angry, etc.).
moods.example: Children should not be blamed for their parents' bad moods.

overadapts.definition: Changes too much to fit what others expect, losing their own self.
overadapts.example: A child who overadapts may struggle to trust their own feelings.
